coredata.dc:title: The synergistic effect of nitrogen and fluorine co-doping in graphene quantum dot catalysts for full water splitting and supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Sim, Yelyn
Kim, Seung Jae
Janani, Gnanaprakasam
Chae, Yujin
Surendran, Subramani
Kim, Hyunkyu
Yoo, Seungryul
Seok, Dong Chan
Jung, Yong Ho
Jeon, Cheolho
Moon, Joonhee
Sim, Uk
coredata.dc:description: Development of efficient electrochemical catalysts is crucial for clean energy technologies such as water splitting, and batteries, etc. Despite large amounts of electrocatalyst research, it remains a challenge to eco-friendly non-metal catalyst showing high performance as much as novel metal. To achieve high performance as much as noble-metal, carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) have been attracted as a candidate to the widespread application using electrochemical reaction. Herein, we report nitrogen, and fluorine co-doped graphene quantum dot (N,F-GQDs) synthesized by two-step functionalizing method for highly efficient overall water splitting. N,F-GQDs demonstrate electrocatalytic effect toward HER, and OER, and is fabricated as two-electrode full-water splitting showing good stability at various potential points. Furthermore, due to fluorine treatment, prepared N,F-GQDs exhibits enhanced electrochemical reaction property, which can induce capacitance due to the large difference of electronegativity between fluorine and carbon. N,F-GQDs is applied for the anode of super capacitance in the three-electrode system and reveal specific capacitance of 270 F g−1 at 1 mV s−1 from CV curves, and GCD capacitance of 244 F g−1 at 3 mA cm−2. This work provides a new avenue for enhancing both activities of the electrocatalytic and electrochemical reaction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen evolution reaction
Tri-functional materials
Heteroatom doping
Electrocatalysts
Supercapacitor

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special issue on Materials Challenges in Alternative and Renewable Energy 2019 (MCARE 2019)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the fabrication process of N,F-functionalized GQDs. Black, red, blue, and purple spheres show carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and fluorine. AFM images of the GQDs dispersed on a SiO2 substrate using a drop-casting method; (b,c) low and high magnification AFM images of GQDs. Scan size, 5 × 5 μm2 and 1 × 1 μm2, respectively. (d,e) Height profiles of selected dashed lines at A and B in b, respectively, showing under 1.5 nm height of GQDs. (f) TEM image of GQDs. TEM grid was supported by monolayer graphene grown by CVD. (g) Histogram showing the size distribution of GQDs. (h) High-resolution TEM image of GQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) wide-scan XPS spectra of GQDs, N-GQDs, and N,F-GQDs. High-resolution (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, and (e) F 1s XPS peaks of GQDs, N-GQDs, and N,F-GQDs.

(a) Polarization curves for HER in a 1 M KOH solution. (b) LSV OER curves for GQDs, N-GQDs, and N,F-GQDs in a 1 M KOH solution. (c) Time dependence of current density for N,F-GQDs at a constant potential of −0.3 V vs. RHE.

(a) Polarization curves for water electrolysis using the N-GQDs, and N,F-GQDs material as both anode and cathode in a 1 M KOH solution. (b) CA curves of the device for 25 h with various applied potential range from 1.6 V to 2.0 V with increase of 0.1 V in basic condition.

(a) CV curves of bare Ni foam, N-GQDs, and N,F-GQDs with different scan rates at 10 mV s−1 at positive potential range. (b) CV curves of N,F-GQDs with different scan rates from 1 mV s−1 to 10 mV s−1 at positive potential range. (c) GCDs curves of N,F-GQDs with different current density at positive potential range.

head.ce:title: The synergistic effect of nitrogen and fluorine co-doping in graphene quantum dot catalysts for full water splitting and supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science & Engineering, Chonnam National University, Gwangju 61186, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science & Engineering, Chonnam National University, Gwangju 61186, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Analytical Science Research, Korea Basic Science Institute (KBSI), Daejeon 34133, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Analytical Science Research, Korea Basic Science Institute (KBSI), Daejeon 34133, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Plasma Technology Research Center, National Fusion Research Institute, Gunsan, Jeollabuk-do 54004, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Plasma Technology Research Center, National Fusion Research Institute, Gunsan, Jeollabuk-do 54004, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of efficient electrochemical catalysts is crucial for clean energy technologies such as water splitting, and batteries, etc. Despite large amounts of electrocatalyst research, it remains a challenge to eco-friendly non-metal catalyst showing high performance as much as novel metal. To achieve high performance as much as noble-metal, carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) have been attracted as a candidate to the widespread application using electrochemical reaction. Herein, we report nitrogen, and fluorine co-doped graphene quantum dot (N,F-GQDs) synthesized by two-step functionalizing method for highly efficient overall water splitting. N,F-GQDs demonstrate electrocatalytic effect toward HER, and OER, and is fabricated as two-electrode full-water splitting showing good stability at various potential points. Furthermore, due to fluorine treatment, prepared N,F-GQDs exhibits enhanced electrochemical reaction property, which can induce capacitance due to the large difference of electronegativity between fluorine and carbon. N,F-GQDs is applied for the anode of super capacitance in the three-electrode system and reveal specific capacitance of 270 F g−1 at 1 mV s−1 from CV curves, and GCD capacitance of 244 F g−1 at 3 mA cm−2. This work provides a new avenue for enhancing both activities of the electrocatalytic and electrochemical reaction.

ce:section.ce:para: Research on renewable and green energy sources have been encouraged to replace fossil fuels that emit air pollutants such as carbon dioxide through the world [1–3]. According to these issues, the electrochemical energy applications such as fuel cell, batteries, and water splitting are receiving more attention compared than ever before [4–8]. For conventional industrialized electrochemical applications, the noble-metal such as Pd, Pt, Au, and Ru are used as electrodes to achieve high performance for fuel cells, water splitting, and batteries [9,10]. Because those noble-metal based materials shows scarcity and high cost, many non-metallic electrode materials have been studied to reach cost-effective and high-performance for widespread of electrochemical applications. As the primary candidate, carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) such as CNT, graphene, and their composites have been extensively researched for decades owing to their interesting properties including high mechanical strength, low densities, thermal and electrical conductivities and stable catalytic activities [11–14].

Among the diverse CNMs, Graphene quantum dots, a few nanometers of graphene particles, have attracted significant attention owing to tunable optical and electronic properties from quantum confinement effect [15–17]. Moreover, it has been reported that oxygen-rich functional groups on their edges have effects on their unique properties, such as luminescence on excitation [18], a tunable bandgap [19], water solubility [20], electrochemical properties [21], and biocompatibility [22]. This has stimulated many efforts to develop various methods to functionalize GQDs, such as plasma treatment [23,24], wet chemistry [25–27], microwave technique [28], etc. Recently, nitrogen functionalized GQDs have been considered as one of the most efficient metal-free catalysts for (photo)electrochemical reactions including HER, OER, and ORR owing to lots of active sites and modified electronic structure [29–32]. In previous reports, carbon adjacent to an N atom can cause a positive shift in Fermi energy, which was a benefit for the charge transfer [9,10]. Furthermore, heteroatoms doped GQDs have been reported as a new electrocatalyst for electrochemical reaction. The theoretical calculation reveals the electronic structure of carbon can be tuned by synergistically coupling effect between heteroatoms [33,34], which triggers a stronger adsorption or desorption sites for the charge transfer.

In this work, we synthesized three types of GQDs with different functional groups, GQDs, N-GQDs, and N,F-GQDs using two-step functionalizing method. We demonstrated electrocatalytic properties of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped GQDs for HER, and OER, furthermore, the prepared samples are applied to the two-electrode full-water splitting system. Due to the co-doping effects, N,F-GQDs also exhibits outstanding supercapacitor properties on positive potential range induced by higher electronegativity between fluorine and carbon electrode, which indicates doping helps electrochemical reaction activity. As a result, this study suggests the possibility of N,F-GQDs as an effective tri-functional catalyst toward full-water splitting and supercapacitors by enhancing both activities of the electrocatalytic and electrochemical reaction.

GQDs were synthesized by a modified Hummer’s method. Chopped carbon fiber (5 g) was placed in an acid mixture H2SO4 (800 mL, 95.0%, Samchun) and HNO3 (200 mL, 60%, Samchun), and it was heated in an oil bath at 90 °C for 48 h with reflux system under vigorous stirring. Subsequently, the solution was cooled to room temperature and diluted with deionized water to a volume of 10-fold. The purification was carried out using dialysis bag until pH 6. The GQDs powder was collected after drying using a vacuum evaporator.

GQDs were functionalized by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) system using ammonia (NH3) gas. The CVD reactor was heated up at 300 °C with flowing 30 sccm NH3 and 70 sccm Ar gases for 2 h. Subsequently, the N-GQDs were fluorinated using dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) plasma reactor at atmospheric pressure. The N-GQDs were placed on a three-step moving stage which was horizontally vibrated at 0.7 mm s−1 for uniform treatment during plasma generation. Input power was maintained at 300 W. After the plasma process, the N,F-GQDs were collected in the output chamber.

Nickel foam was employed as a substrate for all the electrochemical tests. All prepared samples of GQDs, N-GQDs, and N,F-GQDs were loaded on Nickel foam by brush-coating of a homogenous slurry prepared sample. The active material (GQDs, N-GQDs, and N,F-GQDs), carbon black, and polyvinylidene difluoride were mixed in the ratio of 8:1:1 using 0.4 mL of N-methyl 2-pyrrolidine Then, coated electrodes were dried overnight at 80 °C in a vacuum oven. The electrochemical tests were performed in the three-electrode configuration using a Pt wire as the counter electrode, Hg/HgO as the reference electrode, and prepared electrodes as working electrode, respectively. The symmetric water splitting cell consisted of both N,F-GQDs and N,F-GQDs as the positive and negative electrode, respectively. All of the electrochemical tests are performed under basic condition using 1 M KOH aqueous electrolyte.

GQDs were synthesized by chemical exfoliation, a modified Hummer’s method, which was functionalized by chemical vapor deposition system using ammonia gas at 300 °C to generate reactive nitrogen species on the surface of GQDs [35]. Continuously, N,F-GQDs were prepared by directly applying fluorine plasma to N-GQDs powder at ambient pressure, as shown in Fig. 1 a. To study the surface morphology of N,F-GQDs, we dispersed N,F-GQDs on a Si substrate with the drop-casting method. The low and high-magnification of atomic force microscope (AFM) images in Fig. 1b and c show that homogeneous N,F-GQDs particles were uniformly placed on the Si. Furthermore, the height profiles of the N,F-GQDs corresponding to the dashed lines A and B in high-magnification and 3-dimensional AFM images reveal an average under 1.5 nm height of N,F-GQDs, which indicates the number of layers in N,F-GQDs was about ~ 3 layers (Figs. 1d, e and S1). The atomic structures of N,F-GQDs were investigated by low- and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) as shown in Fig. 1f–h. The N,F-GQDs solution was dropped and dried on CVD grew monolayer-graphene supported TEM grid. Most of N,F-GQDs dispersed on graphene sheet show a size distribution from 2 to 10 nm with an average size of 8.7 nm. The lattice structure shown in the high-resolution TEM image indicates the N,F-GQDs are highly crystalline [36].

Nitrogen, fluorine, and oxygen-functional groups on the GQDs were identified by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Strong peaks corresponding to oxygen functional groups CO (1725 cm−1), COH (1245 cm−1), COOH (1398 cm−1), and COC (1039 cm−1) can be identified in the GQDs [37,38], whereas the peak at 1629 cm−1 and three peaks ranging from 2850 cm−1 to 3000 cm−1 can be assigned to CC in-plane stretching and CH vibration mode, respectively (Fig. 2 a) [38]. In case of N-GQDs, the signals from CO, and COOH are weakly detected and red-shifted, indicating that CO and COOH functionalities can be changed to CN, and NCO, respectively, meanwhile, N-H wagging at 870 cm−1 is observed [37,39]. On the other hand, N,F-GQDs shows three additional peaks at around 1250, 1100, and 670 cm−1 assigned to stretching vibration of semi-ionic CF, covalent CF, and CF deformation vibration [38,40].

Wide-scan XPS spectra reveal carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and fluorine peaks at 282.4, 400.8, 532.8, and 686.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 2b). To better understand the chemical bonding composition of three different samples, high-resolution XPS measurement was carried out. The C 1s spectra of GQDs show the peaks at 284.3 and 284.8 eV corresponding to sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons, respectively, whereas the peaks at 285.3 and 289.0 eV are attributed to CO and OCO bond, respectively (Fig. 2c) [34,36]. This is indicative that GQDs contains oxygen-rich functional groups without nitrogen and fluorine species. On the other hand, the C 1s spectra of N-GQDs and N,F-GQDs show that the peak at 286.4 eV attributed to CN bond appears whereas the intensity of carboxyl bonding at 289.0 eV is relatively decreased. Moreover, the N 1s peak exhibits three peaks at 398.5 (pyridinic N), 399.8 (pyrrolic N), and 401.1 eV (graphitic N) [42,43], respectively, which nitrogen is successfully functionalized on N-GQD and N,F-GQDs. In the F 1s spectra, CF bond consisting of covalent CF bond (688. 8 eV) and semi-ionic CF bond (686.2 eV) was only observed in the N,F-GQDs [34,40]. SEM images and EDS maps support that oxygen functional groups exist all the samples, and nitrogen functional groups were found in N-GQDs and N,F-GQDs, whereas fluorine functional groups are only detected in N,F-GQDs. The N,F-GQD was tailored by N (~11 at.%) and F (~9.6 at.%).

These functional groups have a strong influence on the physical properties of GQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the three samples show absorption bands at 231 nm in GQDs, at 262 nm in N-GQDs, and 231 and 262 nm in N,F-GQDs, respectively (Fig. S3). Interestingly, the three different samples emitted different luminescence under irradiation by a 365 nm lamp (6 W). The GQDs and the N-GQDs emitted orange and blue luminescence, respectively, whereas the N,F-GQDs emitted orange and blue luminescence at the same time, which is a great agreement with UV–vis, XPS, FT-IR, and photoluminescence (PL) results. Thus, the chemical bonding configuration has a significant influence on tuning the physicochemical properties of GQDs materials. Based on previous reports, there are three types of CF bond, covalent, semi-ionic, ionic CF bonds. Ionic CF bond has been seldom reported. In this study, CF bonds of N,F-GQDs are composed of covalent C-F bond and semi-ionic bond, a portion of 56.64%, 43.36%, respectively. Nitrogen and fluorine co-doped N,F-GQDs can be expected to enhance electrical and catalytic activities.

The electrocatalytic performance was confirmed to understand the behavior of the catalyst in various catalytic reactions subjected to energy systems. The electrocatalytic properties of the GQDs, N-GQDs, N,F-GQDs were investigated in three-electrode cell configuration using 1 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. The linear sweep voltammetry analysis was carried out at an applied potential ranging from 0 to −0.5 V vs. RHE to evaluate the activity toward hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Fig. 3 a displays the recorded comparative polarization curves of the N,F-GQDs with respect to N-GQDs, GQDs, and bare Ni foam. The LSV curves clearly reveal that the N,F-GQDs exhibit enhanced catalytic activity compared than other samples. The prepared N,F-GQDs electrocatalysts shows the lowest overpotential at 0.13 V where there indicates the current density of 10 mA cm−2, while the N-GQDs, GQDs, and Ni foam require an equivalently high overpotential of 0.25 V, 0.26 V, and 0.29 V respectively. The smallest overpotential value of N,F-GQDs mean that those samples require less amount of activation energy for the evolution of hydrogen compared to N-GQDs, GQDs, and pure Ni foam. The electrocatalytic performance can be strongly affected by the electronic structure of materials. The strong electron withdrawing effect of nitrogen and fluorine atoms can decrease highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level of carbon dots [30,41]. The LUMO level lowered by N, F dopants can be matched with original HOMO level of GQDs which induces hole of LUMO can be recombined with electron of HOMO at the interfacial junction between doped and original domains in the GQDs. The recombination produces useful electron-hole pairs at GQDs-water interfaces and the doped and the original domains serve as n- and p-type domains, respectively. The p-type domain is feasible for electron injection to hydrogen evolution. Similarly, the electrocatalytic activity toward oxygen evolution reaction (OER) was analyzed to examine the bifunctional competency of the prepared electrocatalysts. Compared to the HER, OER is a more energy-intensive reaction, which exhibits some sluggishness in its kinetics. The LSV polarization curves toward OER activity described in Fig. 3b implies that among the prepared electrocatalysts, the N,F-GQDs exhibited an enhanced OER catalytic activity compared than others in the positive potential range between 0 V vs. RHE and 1.8 V vs. RHE. The N,F-GQDs requisite a low overpotential of 0.4 V to generate a current density of 10 mA cm−2, while the N-GQDs and GQDs electrocatalysts required higher overpotential to attain the similar current density. The previous analysis of chemical bonding configuration, N,F-GQDs was confirmed consisting of covalent CF bond and semi-ionic bond. The carbon of semi-ionic CF bond is known to contribute to OER activity because of their positive charge, and this is why the prepared N,F-GQDs shows highest electrocatalytic performance toward OER [44]. Furthermore, the heteroatom doping qualified the doping induced charge redistribution around them, which lowered the overpotential and changed the O2 chemisorption mode to effectively weaken the O-O bonding, facilitating OER at the heteroatom-doped carbon electrodes [45]. Stability is one of the vital parameters to understand the ability of electrocatalysts. To ensure the stability of N,F-GQDs besides its kinetical behavior, the chronoamperometry measurements were performed at −0.3 V vs. RHE for HER and 1.6 V vs. RHE for OER. From the attained result of stability for both reaction, as shown in Fig. 3c, the N,F-GQDs is consistent without any loss of the current density for 80,000 s.

Inspired by the elevated bifunctional electrocatalytic activity of the prepared N,F-GQDs, a lab-scale electrochemical water splitting system was assembled and demonstrated under basic condition using 1 M KOH (Fig. S5). Generally, the full electrochemical water splitting encompasses with the oxygen and hydrogen evolution reaction at the anode and cathode, respectively. The fabricated water splitting system comprises of bifunctional N,F-GQDs employed as an anode and cathode to facilitate both OER and HER activity and compared with asymmetric 2-electrode system consisted of N-GQDs. The LSV polarization curve in Fig. 4 a demonstrates that the fabricated symmetric full-cell system of N,F-GQDs needs a low cell voltage of 1.61 V to generate a water splitting current density of 10 mA cm−2, whereas the N-GQDs (1.66 V), and bare Ni foam (1.8 V) based systems demanded a higher voltage to drive the same current density. Markedly, the number of oxygen and hydrogen bubbles generated at the cathode and anode was more intense in N,F-GQDs system due to high current density under the same potential with other samples. The gas chromatography analysis was also performed to investigate the increasing amount of hydrogen over time from 0 min to 20 min during bulk electrolysis at 1.7 V (Fig. S6). Instigated from the better electrocatalytic performance of N,F-GQDs, stability was measured at diverse potential ranges from 1.6 V to 2 V with an increment of 0.1 V under basic condition using 1 M KOH. The N,F-GQDs has accomplished the stability test for 25 h without any deficiency in the performance (Fig. 4b). Overall, the N,F-GQDs exhibited a low overpotential, high current density, and long-term stability in the alkaline condition, and that enhanced electrocatalytic activity is synergistically increased. This is because that heteroatoms-doped graphene with nitrogen and fluorine generates more number of catalytic active carbon sites near dopants [46,47]. During the catalytic process, the formation of intermediate with adsorbates such as H*, OH*, etc. was considered as a rate determining step. Yue et al. reported that the heteroatom doped graphene effectively lowered the formation energy of intermediates for hydrogen evolution reaction. Particularly, the pyridinic N configuration provided effective catalytic active sites as well as the electronic structure of graphene was modified by dual doping with N and F, which contributes to beneficial sites for the electrochemical process [45]. Thus, N,F-GQDs showed the best catalytic activity for the HER and OER due to synergistic coupling effect of N and F.

Generally, carbon-based materials have the ability to charge and discharge quickly and long cycle life due to the physical adsorption and desorption of electrolyte ions without the chemical reactions [48]. To explore those advantages, N,F-GQDs is applied as the supercapacitor electrode, and it is evaluated using three-electrode set-up in 1 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. Fig. 5 a illustrates the comparative cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of N,F-GQDs, N-GQDs, and bare Ni foam electrodes at a fixed scan rate of 10 mV s−1. Electrochemical capacitors generally can be classified into two categories; (i) electrochemical double-layer capacitors, where the energy storage is based on the electrostatic adsorption of electrolyte ions on the surface area of electrically conductive electrodes, and (ii) pseudo capacitors, where the energy is stored through redox reactions at the electrode/electrolyte interface [49]. Herein, both N-GQDs, and N,F-GQDs electrodes show obvious pseudo capacitive characteristics (Fig. 5a), with a pair of faradaic redox peaks. This faradaic pseudo capacitance occurs simultaneously with the formation of an electrochemical double-layer, and it depends on the chemical affinity of the electrode material with the electrolyte ion and the structure of the N,F-GQDs, and N-GQDs [50]. Notably, the N,F-GQDs electrode delivered over 5 times higher electrochemical capacitance at a similar scan rate of 10 mV s−1 showing values 245.15F g−1 and 47.3F g−1, respectively (Fig. 5a). Hence, the N,F-GQDs was laid open to extensive CV analysis with respect to varied scan rate at a potential ranging from 0 to 0.65 V vs. Hg/HgO. Fig. 5b reveals the CV curves of the prepared N,F-GQDs electrode at variable scan rates ranging from 1 to 10 mV s−1. As a result, the specific capacitance from the CV curve shows 270 F g−1 at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1. The positive shift and negative shift accompanied by the increment of scan rates are observed from the anodic and cathodic peaks, elucidating the diffusion-controlled reaction mechanism involved during the entire process. The galvanostatic charge discharge (GCD) curves of the N,F-GQDs electrode at applied current densities of 3–10 mA cm−2 at potential range from 0 V vs. Hg/HgO to 0.6 V vs. Hg/HgO is shown in Fig. 5c. The non-linear feature of the charge-discharge profiles further indicates the typical pseudocapacitive behavior for both electrodes, in consistent with the CV measurements. Based on the discharge curves, the calculated specific capacitance of N,F-GQDs is 244 F g−1 at a current density of 3 A g−1. Nitrogen doping is helpful to increase the capacitance via surface Faradaic reactions without sacrificing the high rate capability and long cycle life [51]. Furthermore, the high electronegativity difference between fluorine and nitrogen-doped carbon generates positively charged carbons which accelerating the capacitance toward positive potential range [52]. Our electrochemical supercapacitor tests indicate an enhancement of electrochemical activity about prepared N,F-GQDs. These results indicate that the N,F-GQDs is a novel multifunctional catalyst for simultaneous water-splitting and supercapacitor.

In summary, using earth-abundant carbon-based materials, graphene carbon quantum dots (GQDs), nitrogen and fluorine co-doped GQDs (N,F-GQDs) were prepared by plasma treatment. Due to doping effects, N,F-GQDs were consisted of covalent C-F bond and semi-ionic bonds and enhancing both activities of the electrocatalytic and electrochemical reaction. As a result, the prepared sample exhibited overpotential of 0.13 V toward HER and 0.4 V toward OER. (Those potential points indicate the current density of 10 mA cm−2) Moreover, due to enhanced electrochemical reaction activity, it worked as supercapacitor and showed specific capacitance of 244F g−1 at the current density of 3 A g−1.

Yelyn Sim: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Seung Jae Kim: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Gnanaprakasam Janani: Writing - original draft. Yujin Chae: Writing - original draft. Subramani Surendran: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Hyunkyu Kim: Data curation. Seungryul Yoo: Data curation. Dong Chan Seok: Data curation. Yong Ho Jung: Data curation. Cheolho Jeon: Data curation. Joonhee Moon: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Uk Sim: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the Ministry of Science and ICR (NRF-2017M3A7B4049176, 2018R1A5A1025224, and 2018R1C1B6001267). Y.S is supported by Hyundai Moto Chung Mong-Koo Foundation.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent strategies for improving the catalytic activity of 2D TMD nanosheets toward the hydrogen evolution reaction

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MoS2 nanoparticles grown on graphene: an advanced catalyst for the hydrogen evolution reaction
